seoTitle: How to Create a Scrum Board in Jira
metaDescription: Step-by-step guide to setting up a Scrum board in Jira, from creating the project to organizing your backlog into sprints your team can work from.
intro: A Scrum board gives your team a visual way to manage sprints, from planning through to completion. This guide covers setting one up in Jira from scratch, including getting your backlog organized so the first sprint is ready to go.

steps.heading: Create a new Scrum project or board
steps.body: Start a new project using the Scrum template, or add a Scrum board to an existing project if you're just changing how work is visualized.

steps.heading: Set up your backlog
steps.body: Add the issues your team plans to work on into the backlog, which feeds the board once a sprint begins.

steps.heading: Configure board columns
steps.body: Adjust the workflow columns on the board, such as To Do, In Progress, and Done, to match how your team actually moves work forward.

steps.heading: Plan your first sprint
steps.body: Select issues from the backlog to include in your first sprint, then start the sprint so they appear on the active board.

steps.heading: Track progress on the board
steps.body: As work moves forward, team members drag issues across columns, giving everyone a live view of sprint progress.

faq.question: What's the difference between a Scrum board and a Kanban board in Jira?
faq.answer: A Scrum board is built around fixed-length sprints with a backlog you plan from, while a Kanban board shows continuous flow without sprint boundaries.

faq.question: Can I customize the columns on a Scrum board?
faq.answer: Yes, you can add, remove, or rename columns to match your team's actual workflow stages.

faq.question: Do I need issues in the backlog before starting a sprint?
faq.answer: Yes, a sprint pulls from backlog issues, so you'll want your backlog populated and prioritized before starting one.

faq.question: Can multiple teams share the same Scrum board?
faq.answer: It's possible, but most teams find a dedicated board per team keeps sprint planning and reporting cleaner.
